abstract:
Training effective deep learning models for biomedical image segmentation is often hindered by the scarcity of annotated data. In this paper, we introduce "U-Net," a novel convolutional neural network architecture and training methodology designed to operate efficiently with limited training samples. The architecture features a symmetric design consisting of a contracting path to capture global context and an analogous expanding path that enables precise pixel-level localization. By leveraging extensive data augmentation—specifically elastic deformations—the network learns to be invariant to common biological variations while making optimal use of available images. 

Our approach allows for end-to-end training and produces high-resolution segmentation masks. Tested on the ISBI challenge for neuronal structure segmentation in electron microscopy, U-Net outperformed previous state-of-the-art sliding-window convolutional networks. Additionally, the model secured first place in the 2015 ISBI cell tracking challenge for transmitted light microscopy (phase contrast and DIC) by a significant margin. Remarkably efficient, the U-Net can process 512x512 images in less than one second on a modern GPU. We provide our Caffe-based implementation and pre-trained models for public use.
